candidate_problems.text: How can 'counterfactual dependence among parts' be made precise enough to distinguish genuinely load-bearing components from harmless reformulations, redescriptions, or symmetries of the same explanation? The conjecture relies on a notion of non-substitutability, but it is unresolved how to individuate parts and explanations so that changing wording, representation, or equivalent derivations does not falsely count as structural change.

What is the right tradeoff between internal necessity and productive redundancy? The conjecture treats slack and swappability as signs of weaker explanation, but many powerful systems and theories contain robustness, degeneracy, or multiple realizations that preserve explanatory power. An open question is when redundancy is mere ornament versus when it is itself an explanatory virtue that supports generality, error tolerance, or unification.

Can the proposed intervention test—vary one part while holding the explanandum fixed—actually be operationalized without circularity? In many cases, what counts as 'preserving adequacy' or 'the same explanandum' already depends on background theory and explanatory aims. The unresolved problem is to define a non-question-begging procedure for testing load-bearingness across scientific, mathematical, and historical explanations.
